subject: pulmonology
title: Pneumonia
preambleMarkdown:
# Pneumonia

> **Clinical-review notice:** This is a bullet-first examination and clinical-orientation draft. Local protocols, medication doses, procedural thresholds, pregnancy/pediatric pathways, emergency workflows, and jurisdiction-specific guidance must be checked before clinical use.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Pneumonia is an infectious syndrome or organism-related topic in pulmonology.
- This article focuses on airway, ventilation, oxygenation, imaging, pulmonary function, respiratory failure, and prevention.
- The practical clinical priority is host-pathogen interaction, source control, and timely antimicrobial decisions.
- Start by deciding whether the presentation is stable, time-sensitive, complicated, recurrent, or incidental.
- Imported source notes are preserved below for clinical review and citation reconciliation.

sections.heading: Key Points
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Identify unstable, time-sensitive, or function-threatening presentations first.
- Anchor the assessment of Pneumonia to the patient context: age, pregnancy status when relevant, immune status, comorbid disease, medications, prior episodes, and local resources.
- Use focused history and examination before broad testing.
- Choose investigations that change diagnosis, severity assessment, treatment, disposition, or follow-up.
- Treat reversible danger early and give clear safety-net instructions.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Pneumonia is best defined by the specific clinical problem, anatomic site, mechanism, severity, and care setting.
- For this topic, the core concept is host-pathogen interaction, source control, and timely antimicrobial decisions.
- Separate suspected, confirmed, recurrent, complicated, and resolved disease states.

sections.heading: Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Community-acquired, healthcare-associated, travel-related, device-related, or opportunistic infection.
- Localized, disseminated, recurrent, latent, or postinfectious presentation.
- Bacterial, viral, fungal, parasitic, toxin-mediated, or mixed infection.
- Uncomplicated disease, severe disease, sepsis, source-control need, or outbreak/public-health concern.

sections.heading: Epidemiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The epidemiology of Pneumonia depends on age, sex, geography, exposure, comorbid disease, medication use, pregnancy status when relevant, and ascertainment setting.
- In pulmonology, exam questions usually test the common population plus the high-risk exception.
- Risk estimates should be updated from local data or current guideline sources before publication.

sections.heading: Anatomy
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Identify the organ, tissue, vessel, nerve, compartment, joint, pathway, or system most directly involved in Pneumonia.
- Map nearby structures that explain referred pain, spread, obstruction, ischemia, neurologic deficits, imaging findings, or procedure risk.
- For non-anatomic topics, focus on the structure most likely to change symptoms, testing, or treatment.

sections.heading: Physiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Define the normal function that Pneumonia disrupts or depends on.
- Connect the physiology to symptoms, examination findings, laboratory abnormalities, imaging appearance, and treatment targets.
- Consider compensation, reserve, feedback loops, and decompensation triggers.

sections.heading: Pathophysiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The central mechanism in Pneumonia should explain why the patient develops the key symptoms and complications.
- Mechanisms may include obstruction, inflammation, ischemia, infection, immune injury, degeneration, trauma, thrombosis, endocrine imbalance, toxicity, neoplasia, or functional dysregulation.
- Distinguish the primary process from secondary complications and treatment-related harm.

sections.heading: Etiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider the common causes of Pneumonia first, then actively check for dangerous but less common causes.
- Review congenital, acquired, infectious, inflammatory, traumatic, metabolic, neoplastic, medication-related, toxic, pregnancy-related, immune, and idiopathic causes when relevant.
- Use the time course and exposure history to narrow the cause.

sections.heading: Risk Factors
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Risk changes with age, family history, baseline disease, immune status, pregnancy, medications, procedures, travel, occupation, environment, and prior episodes.
- For Pneumonia, risk factors should guide pretest probability and prevention, not replace objective assessment.
- Document modifiable risks that can be addressed during treatment or follow-up.

sections.heading: Genetics
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider inherited disease when presentation is early, familial, recurrent, syndromic, unusually severe, or associated with congenital findings.
- Ask about family history, ancestry when clinically relevant, consanguinity, pregnancy losses, and similar symptoms in relatives.
- Use genetic testing when results change diagnosis, screening, treatment, reproductive counseling, or family risk assessment.

sections.heading: Microbiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider infection when fever, exposure, immunocompromise, procedure, device, wound, systemic inflammation, or rapid clinical decline is present.
- Identify the likely source, route of spread, organism group, need for cultures, need for isolation, and need for source control.
- Antimicrobial decisions should account for severity, host risk, allergies, renal or hepatic function, pregnancy status, local resistance, and prior cultures.

sections.heading: Clinical Presentation
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Fever, localizing symptoms, exposure history, immune status, device or procedure history, and severity define the syndrome.
- Immunocompromised patients, neonates, pregnancy, and older adults may lack classic symptoms.
- Sepsis, airway threat, neurologic involvement, necrosis, and rapidly progressive disease change urgency.

sections.heading: History
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask onset, duration, progression, triggers, exposures, associated symptoms, prior episodes, and treatments already tried.
- Review medications, allergies, procedures, devices, travel, occupation, sexual history when relevant, pregnancy status when relevant, family history, and baseline function.
- For Pneumonia, ask targeted questions that separate uncomplicated disease from dangerous complications.

sections.heading: Physical Examination
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Start with vital signs, mental status, perfusion, respiratory effort, hydration, pain severity, and overall appearance.
- Perform a focused pulmonology examination that directly tests the suspected mechanism.
- Look for systemic involvement, complications, mimics, and findings that change urgency or disposition.

sections.heading: Red Flags
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Shock, altered mental status, respiratory distress, severe hypoxemia, syncope, seizure, major bleeding, or rapidly progressive symptoms.
- Severe pain out of proportion, new neurologic deficit, threatened airway, sepsis, peritonitis, limb ischemia, compartment syndrome, or vision threat.
- Any Pneumonia presentation with organ dysfunction, pregnancy risk, immunocompromise, failed initial treatment, or diagnostic uncertainty needs a lower threshold for escalation.

sections.heading: Differential Diagnosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Compare Pneumonia with disorders that share the same dominant symptom, anatomic site, exposure, laboratory pattern, or imaging abnormality.
- Prioritize dangerous mimics that would be harmed by delay or by the wrong treatment.
- Use acuity, location, risk factors, examination findings, labs, imaging, and response to treatment to narrow the differential.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Approach
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- First decide whether Pneumonia is unstable, complicated, recurrent, incidental, or safe for outpatient workup.
- Use the highest-yield initial test for the suspected syndrome rather than ordering broad panels without a decision point.
- If early tests are negative but suspicion remains high, reassess the history, repeat focused examination, and choose second-line testing based on the remaining differential.

sections.heading: Investigations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Use labs to assess severity, complications, treatment safety, and alternative diagnoses.
- Use imaging, bedside tests, cultures, tissue sampling, functional testing, or procedures when results change management.
- Interpret results with pretest probability and clinical context.
- Avoid tests that add risk, delay, or incidental findings without changing the next step.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Criteria
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Diagnosis of Pneumonia should combine compatible clinical pattern with accepted laboratory, imaging, procedural, histologic, physiologic, or scoring criteria when available.
- Do not diagnose from one nonspecific abnormality when the full syndrome does not fit.
- Document uncertainty and the follow-up plan when the diagnosis remains provisional.

sections.heading: Severity Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Severity depends on stability, organ dysfunction, symptom burden, anatomic extent, complications, treatment risk, and ability to follow up.
- Identify who needs urgent referral, admission, ICU care, procedure, surgery, isolation, rehabilitation, or close outpatient review.
- Use validated disease-specific scoring or staging systems when they exist and are available locally.

sections.heading: Management
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Stabilize sepsis, obtain high-yield cultures when they will not delay care, and start empiric therapy when indicated.
- Narrow therapy once organism, susceptibility, source, and response are known.
- Add source control, isolation, prophylaxis, vaccination, contact tracing, or public-health notification when relevant.

### Initial Management

- Confirm whether Pneumonia is stable, unstable, incidental, recurrent, or complicated.
- Assess airway, breathing, circulation, mental status, pain, hydration, perfusion, and immediate threats.
- Escalate early when red flags, organ dysfunction, procedural need, or diagnostic uncertainty is present.

### Non-pharmacological Management

- Use education, observation, rehabilitation, isolation, procedural planning, or environmental change when it addresses host-pathogen interaction, source control, and timely antimicrobial decisions.
- Tailor activity, diet, wound, exposure, work, driving, return-to-play, or safety advice to the clinical setting.

### Pharmacological Management

- Start empiric antimicrobial therapy promptly when severe infection, sepsis, meningitis, necrotizing infection, or immunocompromised infection is suspected.
- Verify dose, route, duration, interactions, allergies, renal and hepatic adjustment, pregnancy or lactation status, and monitoring.

### Procedures

- Drain abscesses, remove infected devices, debride necrotic tissue, decompress obstruction, or obtain diagnostic samples when source control is needed.
- Check consent, anatomy, imaging, bleeding risk, infection risk, sedation risk, equipment, and aftercare before proceeding.

### Surgical Management

- Surgical or procedural referral is needed for instability, failed medical management, obstruction, perforation, ischemia, uncontrolled bleeding, malignancy concern, repairable structural disease, or unsafe bedside management.
- Timing depends on severity, anatomy, operative risk, patient goals, and local expertise.

sections.heading: Monitoring
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Track symptoms, vital signs, function, pain, intake/output when relevant, focused examination findings, key labs, adverse effects, and response to therapy.
- For Pneumonia, monitoring should detect deterioration, treatment toxicity, recurrence, and missed complications.
- Escalate if expected improvement does not occur or new red flags appear.

sections.heading: Follow-up
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Confirm diagnosis, severity, treatment response, adherence, side effects, and patient understanding.
- Arrange repeat examination, labs, imaging, rehabilitation, surveillance, medication review, or specialty care when indicated.
- Give a clear reassessment interval and explicit return precautions.

sections.heading: Complications
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Complications of Pneumonia may be acute, chronic, recurrent, treatment-related, functional, psychosocial, or procedure-related.
- Prioritize complications that threaten life, airway, limb, vision, fertility, neurologic function, organ function, or public health.
- Document warning symptoms that require urgent reassessment.

sections.heading: Prognosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Prognosis depends on cause, severity, timing of recognition, comorbid disease, age, treatment response, complications, and follow-up reliability.
- Early recognition of dangerous Pneumonia patterns improves disposition and reduces preventable complications.
- Published prognostic statistics should be checked against current specialty sources before release.

sections.heading: Prevention
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Address modifiable risk factors, medication safety, vaccination or prophylaxis when relevant, exposure reduction, screening, and recurrence prevention.
- For Pneumonia, prevention should target the cause, the complication pathway, and the follow-up failure points.
- Use patient-specific counseling rather than generic reassurance.

sections.heading: Patient Education
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Explain Pneumonia in plain language, including what is known, what remains uncertain, and what symptoms should trigger urgent care.
- Review medication purpose, expected benefit, side effects, duration, and what to do if symptoms worsen.
- Confirm the patient understands follow-up, restrictions, wound or device care when relevant, and return precautions.

sections.heading: Nursing Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Monitor vital signs, pain, function, mental status, intake/output when relevant, wounds or devices, and response to treatment.
- Escalate fever, bleeding, respiratory change, neurologic change, perfusion change, uncontrolled pain, medication reaction, or worsening function.
- Reinforce education, discharge instructions, safe mobility, adherence, and follow-up logistics.

sections.heading: Pharmacy Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Check allergies, renal and hepatic function, pregnancy or lactation status, interactions, duplication, contraindications, adherence barriers, and monitoring needs.
- For Pneumonia, medication review should include both treatment drugs and drugs that may cause, worsen, or mimic the problem.
- High-risk drugs require a documented indication, duration, monitoring plan, and counseling.

sections.heading: Dentistry Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask about bleeding risk, anticoagulants, antiplatelets, immunosuppression, prosthetic material, active infection, and relevant medication adverse effects before invasive dental work.
- Coordinate with the treating clinician when medication interruption, prophylaxis, or infection control is being considered.
- Pneumonia does not automatically imply dental antibiotic prophylaxis; use condition-specific indications and local guidance.

sections.heading: Special Populations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Pediatrics: use age-specific normal values, developmental context, weight-based dosing, caregiver education, and safeguarding awareness.
- Pregnancy: stabilize the mother first, choose pregnancy-aware testing and medications when possible, and involve obstetrics for significant disease.
- Older adults: consider frailty, atypical presentation, falls, delirium, kidney function, polypharmacy, cognition, and goals of care.
- Renal or hepatic impairment: adjust diagnostic contrast, medications, dosing, and monitoring.
- Immunocompromised patients: lower the threshold for atypical infection, severe disease, cultures, imaging, admission, and interaction review.

sections.heading: Guideline Comparison
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Current guidance for Pneumonia may vary by country, specialty society, age group, pregnancy status, disease severity, and available resources.
- Compare local policy with major society guidance for diagnostic thresholds, treatment sequencing, drug choice, procedural timing, and follow-up intervals.
- Document where local practice differs from international guidance and why.

sections.heading: Clinical Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Stabilize first when the patient is unstable; diagnostic precision comes after immediate threats are controlled.
- Match each test to a decision it will change.
- Reassess after treatment because nonresponse is often a diagnostic clue.
- For Pneumonia, the highest-yield clinical move is to identify severity and the next safe action.

sections.heading: Common Mistakes
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Treating Pneumonia as a label instead of confirming mechanism, severity, and complications.
- Ordering broad tests without a focused question.
- Missing red flags because the initial symptom sounds common.
- Ignoring pregnancy status, renal function, hepatic function, allergies, interactions, or follow-up barriers.
- Discharging without clear return precautions.

sections.heading: Exam Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Step 1: know the mechanism, anatomy, microbiology, pharmacology, or physiology behind the topic.
- Step 2 CK: know the next best diagnostic or management step.
- Step 3: know disposition, monitoring, complications, follow-up, and patient safety.
- Board exams: know contraindications, high-risk exceptions, and when to escalate.

sections.heading: High-Yield Facts
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Do not delay antimicrobials in unstable sepsis.
- Cultures are most valuable before antibiotics when feasible, but stabilization comes first.
- Source control can matter as much as antimicrobial choice.

sections.heading: Evidence Gaps
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- This Pneumonia draft still needs specialty review before publication.
- Medication doses, procedural thresholds, screening intervals, risk scores, and guideline claims should be localized.
- Imported notes, when present, should be reconciled with current evidence and cited sources.

### Source Excerpt: Pneumonia

_Source lines: 32511-32934._

```text
Pneumonia
A. General Characteristics
1. There are two types of pneumonia: community-acquired and
nosocomial
a. Community-acquired pneumonia (CAP)
- Occurs in the community (infection acquired outside of the
hospital)
- Can be typical or atypical
- Most common bacterial pathogen is Streptococcus
pneumoniae
e. Nosocomial pneumonia (higher risk of multidrug resistance and
nosocomial pathogens)
- Two types: hospital-acquired pneumonia (HAP), and
ventilator-associated pneumonia (VAP)
- HAP: pneumonia that develops >=48 hours after hospital
admission
- VAP: pneumonia that develops >=48 hours after
endotracheal intubation
- Healthcare-associated pneumonia (HCAP) was previously
used to denote pneumonia that occurs in nursing homes or
dialysis centers. This term is no longer used.

- Most common bacterial pathogens are gram-negative
rods (Escherichia coli, Pseudomonas, Klebsiella) and
Staphylococcus aureus

Quick HIT
- "Classic" CAP presents with fever, chills, pleuritic chest pain,
dyspnea, and productive cough.

- The "atypical pneumonia" syndrome, associated with
Mycoplasma or Chlamydia infection, often begins with URI
symptoms (sore throat, rhinorrhea, headache), followed by a
nonproductive cough and dyspnea.

1. There are three recommended methods of prevention
a. Influenza vaccine-give yearly to people at increased risk for
complications and to healthcare workers
b. Pneumococcal vaccine-for patients >65 years and for younger
people at high risk (e.g., those with heart disease, cochlear
implants, sickle cell disease, pulmonary disease, diabetes,
alcoholic cirrhosis, asplenia)
c. SARS-CoV-2 vaccines-indicated for all patients and especially
those with significant comorbidities or advanced age. See
section on COVID-19 for details
Quick HIT
Most cases of CAP result from aspiration of oropharyngeal
secretions because the majority of organisms that cause CAP are
normal inhabitants of the pharynx.

B. Typical CAP
1. Common bacterial agents
a. S. pneumoniae (60%)
b. Haemophilus influenzae (15%)
c. Aerobic gram-negative rods (6% to 10%)-Klebsiella (and other
Enterobacteriaceae)
d. S. aureus (2% to 10%)
2. Clinical features
a. Symptoms
- Acute onset of fever and shaking chills
- Cough productive of thick, purulent sputum
- Pleuritic chest pain (suggests pleural effusion)
- Dyspnea
f. Signs
- Tachycardia, tachypnea
- Late inspiratory crackles, bronchial breath sounds, increased
tactile and vocal fremitus, dullness on percussion

- Pleural friction rub (associated with pleural effusion)

## 3. Chest x-ray (CXR)

a. Lobar consolidation
b. Multilobar consolidation indicates very serious illness
Quick HIT
S. pneumoniae accounts for up to 66% of all cases of bacteremic
pneumonia, followed by H. influenzae, influenza virus, and
Legionella spp.

Quick HIT
Studies have shown that if vital signs are entirely normal, the
probability of pneumonia in outpatients is less than 1%.

C. Atypical CAP
1. Common agents
a. Mycoplasma pneumoniae (most common)
b. Chlamydia pneumoniae
c. Chlamydia psittaci
d. Coxiella burnetii (Q fever)
e. Legionella spp.

f. Viruses: influenza virus (A and B), adenoviruses, parainfluenza
virus, RSV, SARS-CoV-2 (COVID-19, discussed in detail later in
this chapter)
2. Clinical features
a. Symptoms
- Insidious onset-headache, sore throat, fatigue, myalgias
- Dry cough (no sputum production)
- Fevers (chills are uncommon)
e. Signs
- Pulse-temperature dissociation-normal pulse in the setting
of high fever is suggestive of atypical CAP
- Wheezing, rhonchi, crackles
h. CXR
- Diffuse reticulonodular infiltrates
- Absent or minimal consolidation
Quick HIT

"Atypical" pneumonia refers to organisms not visible on Gram stain,
not culturable on standard blood agar, and often intrinsically
resistant to beta-lactams.

Quick HIT
Sputum Culture CAP
- The value of routine sputum collection for Gram stain and
culture is controversial. The Infectious Disease Society of
America has advocated against obtaining routine sputum Gram
stain and culture in adults with CAP managed in the outpatient
setting. For hospitalized patients, sputum collection may be
helpful for severe CAP or when concerned for multidrug-
resistant organisms.

- A good sputum specimen has >25 PMNs and <10 epithelial
cells per low-power field.

D. Diagnosis
1. PA and lateral CXR is the preferred method to confirm diagnosis
(Figure 10-1) (see also Clinical Pearls 10-1 and 10-2).

a. Considered sensitive-if CXR findings are not suggestive of
pneumonia, do not treat the patient with antibiotics.

b. After treatment, changes evident on CXR usually lag behind the
clinical response (up to 6 weeks).

c. Changes include interstitial infiltrates, lobar consolidation, and/
or cavitation.

d. False-negative chest radiographs occur with neutropenia,
dehydration, infection with PCP (Pneumocystis carinii
pneumonia), and early disease (<24 hours).

2. Chest CT is more sensitive for detecting pneumonia than CXR.

Preferred for those with immunocompromised, to look for
complications, and to rule out equivocal cases when pneumonia is
clinically suspected but CXR is nondiagnostic.

3. Pretreatment expectorated sputum for Gram stain and culture-low
sensitivity and specificity, but still worthwhile test if antimicrobial
resistance is suspected.

a. Sputum Gram stain-obtain if severe CAP.

- Commonly contaminated with oral secretions.

- A good specimen has a sensitivity of 60% and specificity of
85% for identifying gram-positive cocci in chains (S.

pneumoniae).

d. Sputum culture-try to obtain in all patients with severe CAP or

those with suspected antimicrobial resistance.

- Specificity is improved if the predominant organism growing
on the culture media correlates with the Gram stain.

4. Special stains of the sputum in selected cases.

a. Acid-fast stain (Mycobacterium spp.) if tuberculosis (TB) is
suspected. If pulmonary TB is suspected, patient should be in
airborne isolation while undergoing TB evaluation.

b. Silver stain (fungi, P. carinii) for HIV/immunocompromised
patients.

5. Urinary antigen assay for Legionella in selected patients.

a. This test is very sensitive.

b. The antigen persists in the urine for weeks (even after treatment
has been started).

6. Consider two pretreatment blood cultures from different sites.

Blood cultures positive in 5% to 15% of cases.

Quick HIT
Patients admitted to the hospital with suspected bacterial
pneumonia should get:
- CXR (PA and lateral)
- Laboratory tests-CBC and differential, BMP
- O 2 saturation
- Two pretreatment blood cultures
- Gram stain and culture of sputum (if severe CAP or concern for
antimicrobial resistance)
- Antibiotic therapy
Quick HIT
Radiographic changes and clinical findings do not help in identifying
the causative pathogen in CAP.

FIGURE
10-1 Chest PA (A) and lateral (B) radiographs: Right lower lobe pneumonia
(straight arrows). On the PA radiograph, the right cardiac border is clearly
visible, and the right hemidiaphragm is partially silhouetted (double straight
arrows). These findings indicate that the infiltrate is posterior or in the right
lower lobe as confirmed on the lateral radiograph (straight arrows).

(Reprinted with permission from Erkonen WE, Smith WL. Radiology 101: The Basics and
Fundamentals of Imaging. Lippincott-Raven; 1998:110. Figure 6-54A and B.)

CLINICAL PEARL 10-1
General Approach to Diagnosis of CAP
The first task is to differentiate lower respiratory tract infection from the
other causes of cough and from upper respiratory infection.

- If nasal discharge, sore throat, or ear pain predominates, upper
respiratory infection is likely.

- Once lower tract infection is suspected, the next task is to differentiate
between pneumonia and acute bronchitis. Unfortunately, clinical features
(cough, sputum, fever, dyspnea) are not reliable in differentiating between
the two.

- CXR is the only reasonable method of differentiating between pneumonia
and acute bronchitis.

CLINICAL PEARL 10-2

Pneumonia Pearls
- In those with alcohol use disorder, think of Klebsiella pneumoniae; in
those with history of travel to or from developing countries, think of TB.

- In nursing home residents, consider a nosocomial pathogen and
predilection for the upper lobes (e.g., Pseudomonas).

- HIV-positive patients are at risk for P. carinii and M. tuberculosis, but are
still more likely to have a typical infectious agent.

- Legionella pneumonia is common in organ transplant recipients, patients
with renal failure, patients with chronic lung disease, and smokers and
presents with GI symptoms and hyponatremia. Legionella pneumonia is
rare in healthy children and young adults.

Quick HIT
Test for microbial diagnosis for outpatients is not required. Empiric
treatment is often successful if CAP is suspected.

Quick HIT
If patient is hypoxic or hypotensive, admit to hospital.

E. Treatment of CAP
1. Decision to hospitalize
a. The decision to hospitalize or treat as an outpatient is one
of the most important decisions and is based on severity of
disease.

b. One common clinical tool is the Pneumonia Severity index
(PSI). Patients are stratified into five classes based on severity.

The PSI can serve as a general guideline, but clinical judgment
is critical in making this decision. The decision to admit the
patient is not based on a specific organism (one does not have
this information when making this decision).

2. Antimicrobial therapy
a. Because the specific cause is usually not determined on initial
evaluation, empiric therapy is often required.

b. For outpatients
- For healthy adults without comorbidities or risk factors for
antibiotic resistance, the most common organisms are S.

pneumoniae, Mycoplasma, Chlamydia, and Legionella. Treat
with amoxicillin, doxycycline, or a macrolide antibiotic
(azithromycin).

- For older adults and patients with comorbidities (heart,
liver, kidney, or lung disease, diabetes mellitus, alcohol use
disorder, malignancy, or asplenia), or those who received
antibiotics in the last 3 months, treat with combination
therapy. Most commonly, amoxicillin/clavulanate PLUS
azithromycin or doxycycline. Or a cephalosporin
(cefpodoxime) PLUS azithromycin or doxycycline.

Monotherapy is an option with a respiratory fluoroquinolone
(levofloxacin), but less optimal due to adverse effects of
fluoroquinolones.

- For outpatients, treatment is continued for 5 days. Generally
treat until patient has been afebrile for 48 hours.

f. For hospitalized patients, a third-generation cephalosporin PLUS
a macrolide (i.e., ceftriaxone plus azithromycin), or a
respiratory fluoroquinolone alone is appropriate.

Quick HIT
For uncomplicated CAP in patients without significant comorbidities,
treat with a beta-lactam such as amoxicillin or amoxicillin/clavulanate.

Add a macrolide (azithromycin) or doxycycline for atypical
coverage.

Quick HIT
For outpatients, treatment is continued for 5 days. Do not stop
treatment until patient has been afebrile for 48 hours.

A 23-year-old man with a history of cystic fibrosis presents to the
emergency department in respiratory failure. He is found to be febrile
with leukocytosis and infiltrates on chest x-ray. Sputum Gram stain
shows gram-negative rods, and culture grows oxidase-positive colonies
that produce a sweet smell. Other significant laboratory values include a
leukocyte count of 17,300/mm 3 and a creatinine of 1.6 mg/dL (baseline
0.9 mg/dL).

Which of the following is the most appropriate antibiotic for this patient?

A. Gentamicin
B. Cefepime
C. Vancomycin
D. Cephalexin
- The answer is B: Cefepime. Pseudomonas is a common respiratory

infection in cystic fibrosis patients and is a major cause of mortality.

It is a common nosocomial pathogen, seen in many cases of HAP and
VAP, and is often MDR. It is also a common infectious agent in any
immunocompromised patient, especially in burn and neutropenic
patients. (C) MRSA is a gram-positive organism, and is not the most
appropriate answer. (A) Aminoglycosides are not the best agent for
this patient for two reasons: First, aminoglycosides are not the
preferred drugs to treat pneumonia; second, the patient is suffering
from acute kidney injury and aminoglycosides have a high rate of
nephrotoxicity. (D) Cephalexin has no activity against Pseudomonas.

F. Treatment of Hospital-Acquired Pneumonia
1. Treatment is tailored toward gram-negative rods, specifically
with antibiotics with antipseudomonal activity (any of the
following are appropriate):
a. Cephalosporins with pseudomonal coverage: ceftazidime or
cefepime
b. Carbapenems: imipenem or meropenem
c. Penicillins: piperacillin/tazobactam
d. Fluoroquinolones: ciprofloxacin, levofloxacin
e. Aminoglycosides: amikacin, gentamicin (usually reserved for
critically ill due to high amount of adverse effects)
f. Polymyxins: colistin (as with aminoglycosides, usually reserved)
2. Macrolides are not used as single-agent therapy
Quick HIT
Pleural effusion is common in patients with pneumonia. Progression
to empyema (infected, loculated pleural fluid) requires chest tube
drainage.

G. Complications
1. Pleural effusion ("parapneumonic effusions")-see Chapter 2.

a. Can be seen in more than 50% of patients with CAP on routine
CXR. Empyema is infrequent in these patients.

b. Most of these effusions have an uncomplicated course and
resolve with treatment of the pneumonia with antibiotics.

c. Thoracentesis should be performed if the effusion is significant
(>1 cm on lateral decubitus film) or patient does not respond
as expected to empiric antibiotics. Send fluid for Gram stain,
culture, pH, cell count, determination of glucose, protein, and
LDH levels.

2. Pleural empyema occurs in 1% to 2% of all cases of CAP (up to 7%
of hospitalized patients with CAP). See Chapter 2.

3. Acute respiratory failure may occur if the pneumonia is severe.

Quick HIT
Lung Abscess Pearls
- The dependent zones of the lungs are most likely to be infected
by aspirated contents-the posterior segments of the upper
lobes and posterior segments of the lower lobes.

- Aspirated material is more likely to affect the right lung because
the right mainstem bronchus is wider, straighter, and shorter.

Ventilator-Associated Pneumonia
A. Patients on mechanical ventilation are at risk of developing
pneumonia because the normal mucociliary clearance of the
respiratory tract is impaired (cannot cough). Also, positive pressure
impairs the ability to clear colonization.

B. Findings to help with diagnosis: new infiltrate on CXR, purulent
secretions from endotracheal tube, fever, worsening hypoxemia, rising
WBC count.

C. Bronchoalveolar lavage (BAL)-bronchoscope passed into lungs to get
cultures.

D. Treatment is with a combination of antibiotics with anti-MRSA
activity (if at risk for MRSA) such as vancomycin OR linezolid, with
one or more of the following drugs with antipseudomonal activity:
1. Cephalosporin with antipseudomonal activity (ceftazidime or
cefepime)
2. beta-Lactam and beta-lactamase inhibitor (piperacillin/tazobactam)
3. Carbapenem (imipenem or meropenem)
4. An additional antibiotic may be added for "double GNR coverage"
if high risk for multidrug-resistant VAP: aminoglycoside OR
fluoroquinolone
```

### Source Excerpt: Pneumonia

_Source lines: 7950-8229._

```text
VIII. PNEUMONIA
A. Discussion
1. Definition. Pneumonia is defined as an infection of the pulmonary
parenchyma.

a. Typical pneumonia refers to pneumonia caused by bacteria; common caus-
ative organisms include Streptococcus pneumoniae, H. influenzae, Staphylo-
coccus aureus, Klebsiella pneumoniae, anaerobes, and Pseudomonas .

b. Atypical pneumonia. Common causative organisms include Mycoplasma
pneumoniae, viruses, Chlamydia pneumoniae, Mycobacterium tuberculosis,
Pneumocystis carinii, and Legionella.

2. Incidence. Pneumonia accounts for 10% of all hospital admissions and is a
leading cause of death in the United States.

3. Causes. The causative agent of pneumonia depends, in part, on host factors.

a. Community-acquired pneumonia is usually caused by Streptococcus pneu-
moniae , M. pneumoniae , viruses, C. pneumoniae, H. influenzae, and, occasion-
ally, Legionella or Staphylococcus aureus.

b. Healthcare-associated pneumonia is usually caused by gram-negative
bacilli, Staphylococcus aureus, or anaerobic oral flora. Less often,
Streptococcus pneumoniae or Legionella is involved.

c. Patients with a history of travel, specific exposures, or certain risk factors
may develop pneumonia caused by other agents (Table 3-5).

Causes of Pneumonia in Patients with Selected
Risk Factors
TABLE 3-5
Risk Factor 	Common Organisms
Age
Infants (1-4 months)
Infants and children younger than 5 years
Older children and young adults
Elderly
Chlamydia trachomatis, Streptococcus pneumoniae,
Haemophilus influenzae
Viruses, S. pneumoniae, H. influenzae
Atypical organisms (e.g., Mycoplasma or viruses) and
S. pneumoniae
S. pneumoniae, influenzavirus, Legionella
Health status
Healthy patients
Debilitated patients (e.g., those in hos-
pitals and nursing homes), neutropenic
patients, patients with cystic fibrosis
Patients with periodontal disease
HIV-positive patients
Patients with sickle cell disease
S. pneumoniae, Mycoplasma, viruses
Gram-negative organisms, especially Pseudomonas
Oral anaerobes
Pneumocystis carinii, Mycobacterium avium-intracellulare,
Mycobacterium tuberculosis, S. pneumoniae, H. influenzae,
Coccidioides immitis, Histoplasma
S. pneumoniae, H. influenzae
Living conditions
Patients in dormitories and barracks 	Mycoplasma pneumoniae, Chlamydia, Mycobacterium
tuberculosis, viruses
Exposures
Contaminated water from air conditioning
units
Cows
Birds
Legionella
Coxiella burnetii
Chlamydia psittaci
Geographical area
Southeast Asia
Ohio and Mississippi valleys
Southwestern United States
Mycobacterium tuberculosis, Paragonimus, Pseudomonas
pseudomallei
Histoplasma, Blastomyces
P U L M O N A R Y E M E R G E N C I E S 	 	99

Pulmonary Emergencies
4. Predisposing conditions include those that impair host defense mechanisms.

a. Failure of the gag or cough reflex may be secondary to altered mental
status, seizures, sedative use, or stroke .

b. Mucociliary clearance is affected by smoking, smog inhalation, alcohol use,
COPD, and viral infection. Patients with cystic fibrosis and chronic bron-
chitis produce thick sputum that is difficult to expectorate.

c. Cellular defense mechanisms can be impaired in patients with AIDS, dia-
betes, asplenia, sickle cell disease, uremia, or malignancy. Chemotherapy
and corticosteroid use can also impair cellular defense mechanisms.

B. Clinical features

## 1. Symptoms

a. The clinical presentation of a patient with pneumonia depends, in part, on
whether the patient suffers from typical or atypical pneumonia. However,
there is considerable clinical overlap.

i. Typical pneumonia is characterized by the abrupt onset of a high fever,
shaking chills, purulent sputum, and significant respiratory complaints
(e.g., cough, dyspnea, pleuritic chest pain). Chest retractions and dia-
phoresis may be evident.

ii. Atypical pneumonia usually has an insidious onset accompanied by
mild respiratory complaints, low-grade fevers, and scant sputum.

b. Some patients may present predominantly with abdominal complaints
(e.g., upper abdominal pain and vomiting), either from irritation of the
diaphragm as a result of lower lobe infection or from the swallowing of
purulent secretions.

c. In elderly patients, the presentation of pneumonia may be more subtle
(e.g., weakness , fatigue, or a change in behavior).

2. Physical examination findings
a. Vital signs. Fever, tachypnea, and tachycardia may be present.

b. Pulmonary examination may reveal decreased or bronchial breath sounds,
rales, dullness to percussion, increased tactile fremitus, and egophony.

c. Cyanosis (if the patient is very hypoxic) may be noted.

C. Differential diagnoses include asthma, COPD, upper respiratory infection, bronchitis,
pneumothorax, pulmonary embolism, foreign body aspiration, and an allergic reaction.

D. Evaluation
1. Pulse oximetry is used to evaluate for hypoxia.

2. Chest radiograph. A chest radiograph is used to diagnose pneumonia, and the
radiographic pattern may be a clue to the causative organism.

a. Classically, lobar infiltrates are seen with S. pneumoniae and Klebsiella infec-
tion. Klebsiella typically involves the right upper lobe and may cause bulg-
ing of the interlobar fissures.

b. Patchy infiltrates, often multilobar and bilateral, are seen with S. aureus and
H. influenzae infection.

c. Interstitial infiltrates are seen with Mycoplasma, Legionella, and viral infections.

d. Cavitation and pulmonary abscesses can be seen with infections caused by
anaerobic organisms, S. aureus, Klebsiella, Pseudomonas , and M. tuberculosis.

e. Pleural effusions are most commonly seen with H. influenzae, Mycoplasma,
and S. pneumoniae infection.

3. Sputum Gram stain. A sputum Gram stain is often helpful in identifying the
causative organism, but its sensitivity is only 40% to 60%.

4. Sputum cultures should be sent if an adequate specimen is available and for
patients admitted to the hospital.

5. ABG. An ABG does not need to be obtained routinely but may be useful in as-
sessing patients with severe respiratory failure caused by the pneumonia.

6. Special tests can be helpful in the diagnosis of certain types of pneumonia.

a. Acute and convalescent Legionella antibody titers and sputum direct fluo-
rescent antibody should be sent for patients with pneumonia thought to be
caused by Legionella.

Quick 	HIT
Legionella pneumonia is
commonly complicated by
gastrointestinal symptoms,
including abdominal pain,
vomiting, and diarrhea.

Quick 	HIT
Endotracheal and
nasogastric tubes bypass
these protective mecha-
nisms and therefore also
predispose to the develop-
ment of pneumonia.

100 	 	S T E P - U P T O E M E R G E N C Y M E D I C I N E

Pulmonary Emergencies
b. Sputum acid-fast bacillus stain and culture should be sent for patients with
suspected pulmonary tuberculosis.

c. Acute and convalescent serum titers as well as cold agglutinins may be
helpful in the diagnosis of M. pneumoniae infection. Acute and convalescent
titers may also be helpful in diagnosing Chlamydia pneumonia.

d. The diagnosis of P. carinii pneumonia can be made by special immunofluo-
rescent stains of sputum from induced specimens or bronchoscopy.

e. Counterimmune electrophoresis can help diagnose S. pneumoniae , H. influ-
enzae , Klebsiella, and Pseudomonas as the cause of pneumonia.

E. Therapy
1. ABCs. Therapy begins with airway management, supplemental oxygen in all
patients, mechanical ventilation in patients with respiratory failure, and cardio-
vascular stabilization.

2. Antibiotic therapy should be initiated early. Empiric choices depend on the
patient's age, underlying medical conditions, specific exposures, clinical and
radiographic presentation, and the results of sputum Gram staining. Recom-
mended antibiotic regimens for the outpatient and inpatient treatment of com-
mon causes of pneumonia are given in Tables 3-6 and 3-7, respectively.

F. 	Disposition

## 1. Admission

a. Patients who are intubated or have the potential for respiratory failure
should be admitted to the ICU.

b. Patients should be admitted to the hospital for intravenous antibiotic
therapy if they meet any of the following criteria:
i. They have a serious underlying disease (e.g., COPD, AIDS, asplenia).

ii. They are older than 65 years or younger than 6 months.

iii. They show signs of toxicity or significant volume depletion.

iv. They are dyspneic or hypoxic on room air.

v. An empyema or abscess has developed.

vi. Dense multilobar involvement is present.

vii. Their living conditions are poor.

2. Discharge. Otherwise healthy adults who are not hypoxic and are well ap-
pearing may be discharged to home. Outpatient therapy with oral antibiotics
should be initiated, and close follow-up is necessary.

Quick 	HIT
The Pneumonia Sever-
ity Index can be used to
estimate mortality rate
and appropriateness of
discharge.

Outpatient Treatment of Community-Acquired
Pneumoniaa
```
